coredata.dc:title: A dual spectroscopic fluorescence probe based on carbon dots for detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol/Fe (III) ion by fluorescence and frequency doubling scattering spectra and its analytical applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jinxia
Bai, Zhangjun
Zu, Fanlin
Yan, Fanyong
Wei, Junfu
Zhang, Saihui
Luo, Yunmei
coredata.dc:description: A convenient, highly sensitive and reliable assay for 2,4,6‑trinitrophenol (TNP) and Fe (III) ion (Fe3+) in the dual spectroscopic manner is developed based on novel carbon dots (CDs). The CDs with highly blue emitting fluorescent were easily prepared via the one-step potassium hydroxide-assisted reflux method from dextrin. The as-synthesized CDs exhibited the high crystalline quality, the excellent fluorescence characteristics with a high quantum yield of ~13.1%, and the narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 6.3±0.5nm. Fluorescence and frequency doubling scattering (FDS) spectra of CDs show the unique changes in the presence of TNP/Fe3+ by different mechanism. The fluorescence of CDs decreased apparently in the presence of TNP via electron-transfer. Thus, after the experimental conditions were optimized, the linear range for detection TNP is 0–50μM, the detection limit was 19.1nM. With the addition of Fe3+, the FDS of CDs appeared to be highly sensitive with a quick response to Fe3+ as a result of the change concentration of the scattering particle. The emission peak for FDS at 450nm was enhanced under the excitation wavelength at 900nm. The fluorescence response changes linearly with Fe3+ concentration in the range of 8–40μM, the detection limits were determined to be 44.1nM. The applications of CDs were extended for the detection of TNP, Fe3+ in real water samples with a high recovery. The results reported here may become the potential tools for the fast response of TNP and Fe3+ in the analysis of environmental pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent
Frequency doubling scattering
2,4,6‑Trinitrophenol
Fe (III) ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence Quenching of CDs by TNP
FDS Enhancement of CDs by Fe3+
Fluorescence Sensing Properties of CDs for TNP
FDS Sensing Properties of CDs for Fe3+
The Fluorescence and FDS Spectra Were Recorded Simultaneously
Mechanism for the Recognition of TNP and Fe3+
Fluorescence Sensing Mechanism of CDs for TNP
FDS Sensing Mechanism of CDs for Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs (inset: the particles size distribution histograms of CDs) (a), FTIR spectrum of CDs (b). XPS survey scan of CDs (c). The enlarged regions for O1s of CDs (d).

Excitation-dependence fluorescence spectra of CDs with excitation wavelength from 310 to 380nm (a); selectivity (black) of CDs in the presence of different ions, nitroaromatic and phenolic species (b); fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of different concentrations of TNP (c); the linear region of F0/F-1 plot with the TNP concentration up to 50μM, inset: the relationship between F0/F-1 and TNP concentration in the range of 0–200μM (d). Error bars were got by repeating experiments for three times and then calculate the standard deviation.

The FDS spectra of CDs with excitation wavelength from 900 to 650nm (a); selectivity (black) and interference (gray) of CDs by Fe3+ in the presence of different metal ion species and nitroaromatic (b); fluorescence spectra of CDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ (c); the linear region of F0/F-1 plot with the Fe3+ concentration from 8μM to 40μM (d). Error bars were got by repeating experiments for three times and then calculate the standard deviation.
Fluorescence spectra of CDs with different concentrations of TNP (0–100μM) and Fe3+ (0–100μM) in aqueous solution excited at 360nm (a) and 900nm (b).
Schematic illustration of CDs as fluorescent probes for the detection of TNP and Fe3+ through fluorescence and scattering spectra.
Determination of TNP and Fe3+ spiked in real water samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ND: not detected. 1 is Haihe River water, 2 is Pan lake water.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different CDs-based TNP and Fe3+ fluorescence probe.
The efficiency for IFE between the CDs and TNP.

head.ce:title: A dual spectroscopic fluorescence probe based on carbon dots for detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol/Fe (III) ion by fluorescence and frequency doubling scattering spectra and its analytical applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes
School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education, Joint International Research Laboratory of Ethnomedicine of Ministry of Education, Zunyi Medical University, Zunyi 563003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education
Joint International Research Laboratory of Ethnomedicine of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A convenient, highly sensitive and reliable assay for 2,4,6‑trinitrophenol (TNP) and Fe (III) ion (Fe3+) in the dual spectroscopic manner is developed based on novel carbon dots (CDs). The CDs with highly blue emitting fluorescent were easily prepared via the one-step potassium hydroxide-assisted reflux method from dextrin. The as-synthesized CDs exhibited the high crystalline quality, the excellent fluorescence characteristics with a high quantum yield of ~13.1%, and the narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 6.3±0.5nm. Fluorescence and frequency doubling scattering (FDS) spectra of CDs show the unique changes in the presence of TNP/Fe3+ by different mechanism. The fluorescence of CDs decreased apparently in the presence of TNP via electron-transfer. Thus, after the experimental conditions were optimized, the linear range for detection TNP is 0–50μM, the detection limit was 19.1nM. With the addition of Fe3+, the FDS of CDs appeared to be highly sensitive with a quick response to Fe3+ as a result of the change concentration of the scattering particle. The emission peak for FDS at 450nm was enhanced under the excitation wavelength at 900nm. The fluorescence response changes linearly with Fe3+ concentration in the range of 8–40μM, the detection limits were determined to be 44.1nM. The applications of CDs were extended for the detection of TNP, Fe3+ in real water samples with a high recovery. The results reported here may become the potential tools for the fast response of TNP and Fe3+ in the analysis of environmental pollutants.

Schematic illustration of CDs as fluorescent probes for the detection of TNP and Fe3+ through fluorescence and scattering spectra.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: There is an increased attention among the environmental scientists for the identification and quantification of traces of harmful substance residues with the fluorescence probes during the past recent decade [1,2]. Nitroaromatic explosives and transition metal ions are two hot issues due to their extensive security and life problems for human health and ecosystem [3–5]. 2,4,6‑Trinitrophenol (TNP), a representative nitroaromatic, is widely used in the rocket fuels, fireworks, and dye industries [6,7]. Apart from its higher explosive power, TNP may cause skin/eye irritation, liver or kidney damage, and anemia through the careless inhale or touch [8]. The good water solubility of TNP can easily cause pollution of soil and aquatic systems [9]. Furthermore, TNP is hardly biodegraded because of its highly electron-deficient nature [10]. On the other hand, as one of the most essential transition metal ions, Fe (III) ions (Fe3+) performs a vital role in many physiological activities of the organism [11,12]. Both the Fe3+ deficiency and overload can disturb the cellular homeostasis and lead to various diseases or even fatal [13]. It is very challenging to selectively detect TNP by fluorescence probes in the presence of competing nitro analytes. Moreover, Fe3+ is a well-known fluorescence quencher due to paramagnetic nature, which makes it difficult to develop a sensitive turn-on fluorescent probe. Even if fluorescence probes including small organic molecules [14–16], quantum dots (QDs), nanoclusters [17,18], and polymer particles [19,20] have been used for detecting TNP and Fe3+ independently. In fact, they still cannot be applied in practice due to some distinct disadvantages of poor solubility, high toxicity and heavily contaminated samples. The new emerged fluorescent nanomaterials carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention owing to their remarkable advances, such as high aqueous solubility, low cytotoxicity, flexible surface modification, excellent photostability and biocompatibility [21–23]. CDs have been broadly used for detecting ionic species [24], organic molecules [25], biomacromolecule, pH and temperature [26–28]. The results of our studies indicated that functional CDs can be used in the respective determination of TNP and Fe3+, the detection limits of our reported methods for TNP and Fe3+ are 0.35μM and 60.9nM, which may not be sensitive enough [29,30]. Although the fluorescence probes have been reported by the scientific community where detection of two even three different kind of analytes is possible at a time [31–34]. Together detection of TNP and Fe3+ using single CDs in the same solvent is still rare, all of them are one-to-one analysis fluorescent probes. Therefore, development of sensing CDs as per the requirement of simultaneous determination TNP and Fe3+ is yet issue for scientists.

With the rapid development of resonance nonlinear scattering (RNLS) methods, great attention has been focused on the frequency-doubling scattering (FDS) technique [35–40]. As a familiar phenomenon in fluorimetric determinations, it appears at the half wavelength of the exciting light when incident light with longer wavelengths passes through a sample. FDS has high sensitivity and superior selectivity for studying the properties and concentration of molecules in very dilute solution. However, there have been no reports of the direct, simultaneous determination of TNP and Fe3+ using fluorescence and FDS spectra. There are still few probes to detect TNP or Fe3+ by FDS. Recently, during the study RNLS of the CDs, we found their maximum FDS scattering peaks were directly proportional to Fe3+ concentration. The FDS intensity was increased greatly, but the spectra of absorption and fluorescence were changed a little. Therefore, it is necessary to study that using CDs to detect TNP and Fe3+ through fluorescence and FDS spectra.

In this work, we report a highly sensitive probe based on CDs for simultaneous detection of TNP and Fe3+ by fluorescence and FDS spectra. CDs were synthetized from dextrin via a simple potassium hydroxide-assisted reflux process. This probe can be employed for the sensitive and selective detection of TNP by fluorescence turn-off. The fluorescent intensity of CDs at 442nm decreased with the growing concentration of TNP in the range of 0–50μM, the detection limit was 19.1nM. Meanwhile, the CDs by contacting Fe3+ lead to the remarkable enhancement of FDS, the RRS and SOS intensities of the CDs solution were very weak. The detection limit was 44.1nM for Fe3+ by FDS method, which is 1.4-fold lower than our previous reports [2]. Our experiment discovered that fluorescence and FDS spectral responses appeared completely independent of each other. The mechanism was also discussed in paper. Furthermore, CDs can be applied for the selective determination of TNP and Fe3+ in water samples. To our knowledge, we report a fluorescence and FDS spectral sensing method for the simultaneously detection of TNP and Fe3+ based on CDs for the first time.

All materials and chemicals were of analytical grade and used as purchased without further purification. Nitro Benzene (99%), 2,6‑dinitro toluene (97%), 1,3‑dinitro benzene (98%), 3,4‑dinitro toluene (98%), 3,5‑dinitro benzoic acid (97%), and 2,4,6‑trinitrophenol (98%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Pyrocatechol (99%), hydroquinol (99%), resorcinol (99%), bisphenol A (97%), 5‑methyl‑3‑benzenediol (98%), dopamine (97%) and Quinine sulfate (95%) were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). Dextrin (96%) and potassium hydroxide (90%) were supplied by local companies Tianjin Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). The standard Fe3+ and other metal ions solutions with concentration 2mM were prepared by dissolving appropriate amount of corresponding AAS grade nitrate with distilled water for further experiments. Triple-distilled water was used to prepare all solutions and subsequent dilutions throughout the experimental process.

The flourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a TENSOR37 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer from Tianjin Gangdong scientific and technological development Co., Ltd. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on a H-7650 electronic microscope of HITACHI from Japan. Fluorescence emission spectroscopy was carried out on an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Tianjin Gangdong scientific and technological development Co., Ltd. Zeta potential measurements were conducted using a Malvern, Nano ZS 90 zetasizer. A PHS-3W pH meter (Shanghai Lida instrument factory) was utilized to measure the pH values of aqueous solutions.

CDs were synthesized by one-step potassium hydroxide-assisted reflux method. Dextrin (1.0g) was treated with hot water (80–90°C) (25mL). Then 0.1M potassium hydroxide (25mL) was added to the hot dextrin solution. The solution became brown gradually after refluxing for 12h at 120°C. After cooling down, the insoluble precipitate in the solution was removed by centrifugation and the supernatant was collected. The solution was subsequently neutralized (pH=7.0) by 0.1M hydrochloric acid. To remove the impurities, the solution was given a dialysis treatment with dialysis bag (Mw=3000) for 4days. At last, the obtained solution changed to transparent yellow color. Eventually, pure CDs were obtained by rotary evaporation. The solution inside the dialysis bag was concentrated and dried to obtain the brown CDs powder.

The fluorescence quantum yield was determined by slope method by the reference of quinine sulfate: compared the integrated photoluminescence intensity (λex =360nm) and the absorbance value (several values gave the curve) of the CDs samples with that of the references. Then used the equation [41]:
(1)
φ x
=
φ st
K x
K st
η x
η st
2
where φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, K is the slope determined by the curves and η is the refractive index. The subscript “st” refers to quinine sulfate and “x” refers to the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Quenching of CDs by TNP
ce:section.ce:para: To negate the effect of pH on the fluorescence of CDs, they were dispersed in Tris-HCl buffer (0.1M, pH=7) to carry out all sensing experiments. All the standard solution of phenols samples was prepared by dissolving them in ethanol with a final concentration of 1mM, respectively. Then 0.2mL 1mgmL−1 CDs solution and 0.2mL phenols solutions were mixed to form a 10mL clear solution with water, respectively. After mixing completely, the fluorescence spectra were detected at 360nm excitation wavelength. To conduct the TNP test, 0.2mL CDs solution and different amounts of TNP were mixed to form a 10mL clear solution with water. Fluorescence spectra were tested the same as the above process. To investigate the interference of other phenols, 0.4mL other phenolic species were added into the mixture of 0.2mL 1mgmL−1 CDs solution and 0.2mL TNP to form a 10mL clear solution with water. Then the fluorescence spectra were tested after mixing absolutely. The selectivity and sensitivity measurements were repeated thrice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FDS Enhancement of CDs by Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
Various metal ions solution of the concentration of 2mM were prepared, including Na+, K+, Ba2+, Ce3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pd2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Al3+, and Pb2+. In a typical detection experiment, 0.2mL 1mgmL−1 CDs solution was added into 0.2mL metal ions solution to form a 10mL clear solution. After mixing completely, The FDS intensity (I
FDS ) of the system were recorded at λem =1/2λex, respectively. The FDS spectra were obtained by plotting the FDS intensities vs. the different wavelengths. FDS spectra were detected at λex =900nm excitation wavelength. For Fe3+ detecting, 0.2mL CDs solution and different amount of Fe3+ were mixed to test the sensitivity. In order to find the interference of other common metal ions toward Fe3+ detection, 0.4mL metal ions solutions were mixed with the solution of 0.2mL CDs and 0.2mL Fe3+. Then FDS spectra were tested the same as the above process.

CDs were easily prepared through a novel green potassium hydroxide-assisted Dextrin carbonization route. Compared with the hydrothermal method, the direct carbonization is more rapid and simple. Most importantly, it has been identified that the CDs can only be obtained by the carbonization method.

The morphologies of the prepared CDs were investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, CDs are near spherical in shape and uniformly distributed without aggregation in water. Their size distribution ranges from 2.0nm to 9.0nm (Fig. 1a, inset) with a maximum population at about 6.3nm±0.5nm, and this is similar to that of C-dots as reported elsewhere [42].

In order to provide proof for various chemical bonds, the surface functional groups of CDs were characterized by FTIR (Fig. 1b). The two peaks at 3432cm−1 (broad absorption peak) and 1663cm−1 (weak absorption peak) are assigned to the stretching vibrations of the carboxy OH, CO group. The FTIR spectrum also shows the characteristic COC stretching vibration which centered around 1022cm−1. The bands around 3151cm−1 and 1403cm−1 are originated from the stretching and rocking vibrations of CH. The carbonyl and hydroxyl groups on the CDs might give rise to their high water-solubility, corresponding with their zeta potential (−21.7mV), CDs are suitable for effective interaction with TNP and Fe3+. In addition, there wasn't any precipitation in CDs solution for three months at 4°C in the refrigerator, which revealed their excellent stability (dispersible), it is beneficial for TNP and Fe3+ detection in complex samples.

Besides, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was taken to analyze the composition and surface elements of CDs (Fig. 1c). The XPS survey spectrum of the CDs shows two main peaks at 285 and 532eV, which are ascribed to C1s and O1s, respectively. The spectrum of O1s is split into three peaks coming from the binding energy of CO, COH and COC (Fig. 1d). The results are almost the same as the FTIR spectra conclusions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Sensing Properties of CDs for TNP
ce:section.ce:para: In order to reveal the optical properties of CDs, the fluorescence spectra of CDs were studied. The fluorescence emission of the CDs shows a similar property of excitation-dependence to those in many previous reports (Fig. 2a) [43,44]. The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated to be 13.1%, using quinine sulfate (54% in 0.1molL−1 H2SO4, λex =360nm) as a reference material. The CDs show the emission peak at 442nm under 350nm.

In order to evaluate the spectral effects of various nitroaromatic and phenols on aqueous dispersion of CDs, the fluorescence responses of CDs toward five nitroaromatic and five common phenols were investigated under 350nm. As can be observed in Fig. 2a, among the studied molecules, only TNP caused a fluorescence quenching. Additionally, the possible interferences of coexisting compounds in TNP detection were also tested. It is clear seen that the fluorescence intensity of CDs shows no change or a slight change after the addition of competitive compounds. Moreover, the metal ions are also used to investigate the selectivity of TNP detection. All the other metal ions, include Fe3+, have no influence on fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence of TNP (Fig. 2b). Then it was investigated that the efficiency of CDs for the detection of TNP (Fig. 2c). Accompanied with the increase of TNP concentration in the range of 0μM–200μM, the fluorescent intensity of CDs at 442nm decreased in proportion. The blue fluorescence of CDs is gradually weakened, which supplied a colorimetric assay for TNP detection. The change of the fluorescence shows linear relationship with the concentration of TNP in the range of 0μM–50μM with a square of correlation coefficient of 0.99 (Fig. 2d). The quenching process can be fitted by the Stern-Volmer equation [45]:
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K sv
C
wherein, C is the concentration of TNP; and F0 and F are the steady-state fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of TNP, respectively. In this case, the Stern-Volmer constant Ksv was calculated to be 0.03235μM−1. The limit of the detection was calculated to be 19.1nM based on 3×δblank /k (where δblank is the standard deviation of the blank solution and k is the slope of the calibration plot).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FDS Sensing Properties of CDs for Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The FDS spectra of CDs were recorded by synchronous scanning at the different incident wavelengths after 10min. The results are shown in Fig. 3a. The FDS emissions load in the range from 325nm to 450nm when the CDs are excited at long wavelength from 650 to 900nm, the maximum wavelength (λex/λem) of FDS as the detection wavelength was located at 900nm/450nm.

The influences of coexistent foreign substances such as metal ions, five nitroaromatic and five common phenols were tested in the presence of 0.2mL 1mgmL−1 CDs. As displayed in Fig. 3b, it is very obvious that the FDS intensity was only sensitive to Fe3+.

To further investigate the interaction of CDs with Fe3+ in Tris-HCl buffer (0.1M, pH=7) solution, the FDS titration experiments were then performed. An increase of FDS intensity of CDs could be observed with gradual addition of Fe3+. As seen from Fig. 3c, upon the gradual addition of Fe3+ under excitation at λex =900nm, an emission band peaked of CDs at nearby 450nm significantly increased in FDS, reaching saturation at addition of 200μM. The linear FDS enhancement of CDs (10μM) toward amounts of Fe3+ added was obtained over the range of 8–40μM (R2 =0.99) (Fig. 3d). These revealed the detection limits of CDs for Fe3+ around 44.1nM, which was sufficiently low for the detection of Fe3+ found in many chemical and biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Fluorescence and FDS Spectra Were Recorded Simultaneously
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore whether our method has specificity for TNP and Fe3+ respectively, we investigate the interference between them. Fig. 4a shows that the fluorescence of CDs at 442nm reduces markedly in the presence of TNP. Then the fluorescent probe was excited at 900nm to detect Fe3+, as shown in Fig. 4b. When the CD solution is gradually added TNP and Fe3+ simultaneously, the fluorescence at 450nm enhances greatly the same as the result above. These phenomena demonstrate that the CDs can detect TNP and Fe3+ respectively by different excitation wavelengths when they coexist. These results suggest that the CDs may be used as a dual-channel probe for applications in environmental and energy problems.

To verify the accuracy of the fluorescence and FDS detections methods, two water samples from Haihe River (Tianjin, China) and Pan lake water (Tianjin, China) were collected for determination of TNP and Fe3+.Two water samples were stood for 24h to precipitate and the supernatant was taken out for further analysis. As for spiked tests, appropriate amounts of TNP and Fe3+ standard solutions were added to the water sample, the fluorescence and FDS detections were conducted under the same conditions. The relative standard deviation (RSD) and recovery were tested by a standard addition method. The results are shown in Table 1 . the recoveries varied in the range of 96.0–104%, suggesting the applicability of the proposed method to real samples.

In addition, this dual spectroscopic fluorescence probe exhibited excellent detection limit when compared to other methods published, implying the CDs have very promising application in the detection of TNP and Fe3+ (Table 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the Recognition of TNP and Fe3+
Fluorescence Sensing Mechanism of CDs for TNP
ce:section.ce:para: In general, fluorescence quenching can be caused by dynamic quenching, static quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE) and electron transfer [50]. For dynamic quenching or static quenching, the Stern-Volmer constant Ksv can theoretically described to determine which type of quenching is observed. Ksv is affected by the change of the temperature. The values of Ksv increase with the increasing of the temperature for dynamic quenching, the values of Ksv decrease with the increasing of the temperature for static quenching. In this work, Ksv is calculated at different temperatures, as shown in Table S1. It can be found that the change of the values of Ksv is ignorable. These results confirmed that the TNP-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs could not be attributed to dynamic quenching or static quenching.

The fluorescence quenching may be attributed to the FRET process from CDs to TNP, it needs that the emission spectral of CDs overlaps the absorbance spectral of TNP. As depicted in Fig. S1, the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs has a small degree of overlap with the absorbance of TNP, which indicated that CDs and TNP cannot constitute a donor-acceptor pair of a FRET system. So, the fluorescence quenching of CDs may not be attributed to the FRET mechanism.

Moreover, from the Fig. 3, it can be observed that the excitation spectrum of CDs overlaps the absorbance spectrum of TNP, which meets the condition of IFE. In order to investigate the quenching mechanism of CDs, the absorption spectra of TNP and fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured then the value of Fcor/Fobsd was calculated by the Parker equation [51].
(3)
F cor
F obsd
=
2.3 d
A ex
1 −
10
− d
A ex
10
g
A em
2.3 s
A em
1 −
10
− s
A em
where Fobsd is the observed fluorescence intensity of CDs with the addition of TNP at 420nm, Fcor is the corrected fluorescence intensity after removing IFE from Fobsd. Aem and Aex are the absorbances of CDs at the maximum excitation wavelength (ƛem) and maximum emission wavelength (ƛex) with the addition of CDs at 320nm and 420nm. “s” is the thickness of the excitation beam. “g” is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of cuvette, “d” is the width of the cuvette. In this case, s=0.10cm, g=0.4cm, d=1.00cm. The correction factor (CF) of the IFE (Fcor/Fobsd) at each concentration of TNP could be calculated.

As shown in Table 3 , with the concentrations of TNP increasing from 0 to 200μM, the effect of IFE increases with the increasing of the value of Fcor/Fobsd. The value of CF increasing from 1.02–1.32, the efficiency for IFE is 0.32. As reported [52], the IFE may be the main effect which leads to the quenching of CDs by TNP, there are also some other quenching mechanism coexisting.

To the best of our knowledge, the origin of the highly selective quenching by TNP can be partly attributed to the electron transfer from excited state of the electron donating CDs to electron withdrawing TNP molecules. Because nitro groups are the typical electron withdrawing substituents that could enhance the oxidative ability of the aromatic ring, or the electron affinities of TNP evaluated from its LUMO energies are higher than other electron-deficient analytes [53,54]. That is, in the presence of TNP, the excited electron of CDs would undergo a transfer to TNP, instead of relaxation to the ground state with fluorescence emission. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs by TNP both could be presumably dominated by IFE and electron transfer (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: FDS Sensing Mechanism of CDs for Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the detection mechanism of CDs toward Fe3+detection, the scattering intensity has been calculated by the following equation [55]:
(4)
I =
24
π 3
v 2
N
λ 4
n 1 2
−
n 0 2
n 1 2
+ 2
n 0 2
2
I 0
where I and I
0 represent the intensity of scattering light and incident light, v is the volume of one particle, N is the number of the scattering particles per unit volume, n
1 and n
0 are the refractive indexes of the particle and the surrounding medium, respectively. When the experimental conditions (I
0 , N, n
1 and n
0 ) are fixed, the scattering intensity I is direct proportional to the square of the volume for scattering particle.

In this work, the plentiful hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs exhibited good binding affinity for Fe3+. The as-formed CDs-Fe3+ nanoparticles would facilitate charge average diameter, leading to the formation of larger size particles (as shown in Fig. S2). Additionally, the mean zeta potential for the CDs also dramatically changed from a negative value (−21.7mV) to a positive (3.52mV) with varying Fe3+ concentrations from 0 to 100μM. The repulsive force is weakened due to zeta potential charge, and the CDs can come close enough together to agglomerate into larger particles. As a result, the FDS intensity enhanced.

The FDS spectra can also be described by the following equation [56]:
(5)
I
λ
λ ex
= Kb cf
d
f
Δλ
E m
λ
E x
λ ex
when λex =2λ and λex =1/2λ, the I ~λ relation curves are just the FDS and SOS spectra. That is,
(6)
I FDS
= Kb cf
d
f
Δλ
E m
λ
E x
2 λ
(7)
I SOS
= Kb cf
d
f
Δλ
E m
λ
E x
1 / 2 λ
where K is the ratio coefficient, b the thickness of the cuvette, c the concentration of the scattering particle, d the size of the scattering particle, Δλ the difference of excitation and emission wavelengths, Em(λ) the intensity distribution of the scattering light, Ex(λex) is the excitation spectrum of the scattering light.

From Eqs. (6), (7), it can be seen that when other conditions are kept constant, the FDS and SOS intensities, I FDS and I SOS, are proportional to the size of the scattering particles. Particularly as FDS is produced by using the incident light of long wavelength, its incident energy is lower than that of SOS. Therefore, the SOS intensity is not sensitive for the addition of Fe3+, the FDS method is more highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+.

In summary, we report a difunctional CDs for the differential detection of TNP and Fe3+ by the fluorescence and FDS spectra. CDs were synthesized via a facile and one-step potassium hydroxide-assisted reflux method using dextrin. These unique CDs possessed highly selectivity and satisfactory recovery for TNP detection in complex water samples based on the IFE of the excitation and emission bands of CDs by TNP and electron transfer from CDs to TNP, the detection limit for TNP was 19.1nM, with the linear range of 0–50μM. Furthermore, with the addition of Fe3+, the emission peak for FDS at 450nm was enhanced under the excitation wavelength at 900nm. The main cause of the enhancement of FDS intensity comes from the combination of CDs and Fe3+. CDs come close enough together with Fe3+ significantly increased the particles size, resulting in the enhancement of FDS. Particularly as FDS was produced by using the incident light of long wavelength, its incident energy is lower. Most importantly, coexisting substances have no influence on the fluorescence and FDS spectra of CDs, which is very different from previously reported CDs. Therefore, the FDS method appeared to be highly sensitive with a quick response to Fe3+.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21374078, 51678409), and Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (No. 15JCYBJC18100, 15ZCZDSF00880), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507) and The Science and Technology Plans of Tianjin (No. 16PTSYJC00110).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.03.080.
